statement.markdown: Implement the Fibonacci sequence using Dynamic Programming. Given a positive integer `n`, return the `n`-th Fibonacci number. However, the implementation has a logical bug and does not produce the correct results for larger inputs.
statement.inputFormat: Input consists of a single integer, `n`, representing the index of the Fibonacci number to return.
statement.outputFormat: Output should be a single integer representing the `n`-th Fibonacci number.
statement.notes: Your implementation should use Dynamic Programming to improve performance. Time complexity should be O(n), and space complexity should be O(n) as well.

constraints.description: Index of the Fibonacci number to return

hints.text: Review the Fibonacci sequence definition and ensure your implementation follows it correctly.

editorial.title: Fibonacci using Dynamic Programming
editorial.intuition: The Fibonacci sequence is defined recursively, which can lead to redundant calculations. We can use Dynamic Programming to store intermediate results and avoid redundant calculations.
editorial.bruteForce: A naive recursive implementation would have a time complexity of O(2^n) due to redundant calculations.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a bottom-up approach to calculate the Fibonacci numbers iteratively, storing each result in a table for future reference.
editorial.optimalSolution: Our optimal solution uses a dynamic programming table to store the Fibonacci numbers, achieving a time complexity of O(n) and space complexity of O(n).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Our solution is correct because it iteratively builds up the Fibonacci numbers in the table, ensuring that each result is calculated only once.
editorial.dryRun: Here's an example dry run for the input n = 5: [0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5]

starterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: int fib(int n) { // TODO: implement } };
starterCode.java: public class Solution { public int fib(int n) { // TODO: implement } }
starterCode.python: class Solution: def fib(self, n: int) -> int: pass

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: int fib(int n) { if (n > 1) return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2); return 1; } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public int fib(int n) { if (n > 1) return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2); return 1; } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def fib(self, n: int) -> int: if n > 1: return self.fib(n-1) + self.fib(n-2); return 1
buggyStarterCode.javascript: var fib = function(n) { if (n > 1) return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2); return 1 }

solutions.cpp: class Solution { public: int fib(int n) { if (n <= 1) return n; vector<int> dp(n + 1); dp[1] = 1; for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) dp[i] = dp[i - 1] + dp[i - 2]; return dp[n]; } };
